report_name: Microsoft Threat Report Reveals QR Code Phishing as Fastest-Growing Email Threat, Surging 146% in Early 2026

attack_patterns.name: Phishing
attack_patterns.description: Attackers are increasingly using QR code-based phishing (quishing) delivered via email to steal user credentials. The QR codes, often embedded in PDF attachments, lead users to malicious landing pages.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Link
attack_patterns.description: Malicious QR codes are used in place of traditional hyperlinks within emails and attachments to deliver users to phishing sites, bypassing URL scanners.

attack_patterns.name: Malicious Link
attack_patterns.description: Users are socially engineered to scan a malicious QR code within an email or PDF attachment, which directs their mobile device's browser to a credential harvesting page.

attack_patterns.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: Malicious URLs are obfuscated from email security scanners by being embedded as QR codes within PDF attachments. Attackers also use CAPTCHA-gated landing pages to prevent automated analysis of the final phishing site.

tools.name: Tycoon2FA
tools.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform that provides attackers with ready-made phishing kits and infrastructure, including mechanisms to bypass multi-factor authentication.

tools.name: Kratos
tools.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform mentioned as part of the ecosystem that industrializes and scales phishing attacks.

tools.name: EvilTokens
tools.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform mentioned as part of the ecosystem that industrializes and scales phishing attacks.

identities.name: Global Organizations
identities.description: A large-scale QR code phishing campaign in March 2026 targeted 179,000 organizations across 43 countries.

infrastructure.name: QR Code Phishing Landing Page
infrastructure.description: Malicious credential harvesting pages, often cloning legitimate services like Microsoft 365, designed to steal user credentials after they scan a QR code from an email.

infrastructure.name: CAPTCHA-gated Phishing Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Phishing landing pages that use legitimate CAPTCHA services to evade automated URL scanning tools and add a layer of perceived legitimacy before presenting the credential theft form.

indicators.name: Potential CAPTCHA-Gated Phishing Page Access
indicators.pattern:
title: Potential CAPTCHA-Gated Phishing Page Access
id: 2b8a1c9d-5e7f-4b1a-9c8d-3e4f5a6b7c8d
status: experimental
description: >
  Detects web requests to URLs containing CAPTCHA-related keywords. Threat actors use CAPTCHA gates on phishing pages to evade automated URL analysis tools. While legitimate sites use CAPTCHA, their presence in a redirect chain from an email link can be suspicious.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/04/30/email-threat-landscape-q1-2026-trends-and-insights/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-01
tags:
  - attack.t1027
  - attack.defense_evasion
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate access to websites that use CAPTCHA for bot protection.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|contains:
      - 'captcha'
      - 'recaptcha'
      - 'hcaptcha'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects web traffic to pages containing CAPTCHA elements, a technique used in modern phishing to block automated analysis tools from reaching the final credential harvesting page.
